video_title: Jake Paul's Business Partner on Bitcoin, AI & the Next Trillion-Dollar Trade
gist: Jeff Woo, managing partner at Antifund (Jake Paul and Logan Paul's investment firm) and co-founder of Ketone IQ, lays out a 'choke-point' investing framework spanning AI compute/memory, defense tech, energy commodities like tungsten, and the human-data economy, revealing that Sam Altman told him at a dinner that OpenAI bought roughly 40% of the world's memory supply after a trip to Korea. He also discloses he is now trimming his long-held Bitcoin position in favor of AI exposure and Hyperliquid.

insights.claim: Jeff Woo traces the downstream capital chain of an AGI investment thesis: capital first bids up OpenAI/Anthropic, then flows down to Nvidia and semiconductor chips, then further downstream into power infrastructure and rare-earth minerals.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Investors can systematically follow an AGI-belief chain downstream from model labs to chips to power to raw materials to find underpriced links.
insights.quote: obviously people are bidding up open anthropic and then you go down to Nvidia and semiconductor chips and I think it's increasingly valuable and clear that you go down to power you go go down to rare earth minerals

insights.claim: Jeff Woo uses a 'three strikes and I'm in' heuristic: when he hears the same investment idea independently from three unconnected people, he treats that convergence itself as a strong buy signal rather than requiring further diligence.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Retail or smaller investors can replicate this by tracking when a niche thesis starts surfacing from multiple independent, credible sources.
insights.quote: I do have a three strikes and I'm in. Uh if I hear the same thing from three people who don't know each other, I just learned like don't ignore it.

insights.claim: Woo builds his investment universe bottoms-up via 'mosaic theory' — talking directly to founders working on robotics, mining, and fusion research to identify what is underpriced, rather than relying on public information or sell-side research.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Signals that the most differentiated alpha in AI/hard-tech investing currently comes from direct founder access rather than public data.
insights.quote: talk to all the smart people in the world doing all the interesting things on robotics, mining stuff, doing fusion research, making robotics actually happen and just build up a bottoms up universe

insights.claim: According to Woo, hedge fund managers who became crossover investors into private markets did so primarily to harvest information advantage, not direct private-market returns, because private-market developments often led public-market moves before quarterly disclosures caught up.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Public-market investors without private access are structurally behind information that later shows up in earnings; crossover/hybrid strategies are a way to close that gap.

themes.theme: AI Value Chain: Foundation Labs vs. Vertical Specialists

insights.claim: Woo expects foundation model labs like OpenAI and Anthropic to increasingly verticalize into application-layer businesses (e.g., legal tech, medical tech, finance, drug discovery spinouts) rather than remaining purely horizontal model providers, citing that they are already hiring PMs and domain experts like investment bankers to pursue specific verticals.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Startups building thin wrappers on foundation models in large verticals face direct competitive entry risk from the labs themselves.
insights.quote: I think the foundation labs are going to verticalize and do application layer stuff.

insights.claim: Woo estimates the stable long-term equilibrium is roughly a 50/50 split of AI applications between foundation labs and the startup ecosystem, a moderation from an earlier fear that big labs would capture around 80% of the most useful applications and leave startups only 20%.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Investors betting on vertical AI startups should size positions assuming labs will contest roughly half of the application layer, not assume startups get a clear field.
insights.quote: I think there is a universe where the big boys control like 80% of the most useful applications and they'll let some startups play on the platform for like 20% of the applications.

insights.claim: Woo argues vertical AI startups' real defensibility comes from an 'unfair distribution advantage' — proprietary personal expertise, data, and an existing audience (e.g., a well-known financial personality's following baked into a product like Sylvia) — not from model quality, since generic frontier models are becoming commoditized.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: When evaluating vertical AI startups, investors should weight distribution/data moats over raw model capability, since the latter is quickly replicated by foundation labs.
insights.quote: you're a financial expert. You're like a guru in this space. You have a big following. So I think you have a lot of credibility to say that my personal insights, the data, the network that I have is being baked into Sylvia.

insights.claim: Both startups and foundation labs are racing to accumulate proprietary data fast enough to reach flywheel 'escape velocity' before the other side, a race intensified by big labs now spending billions of dollars directly on data acquisition.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Data-moat speed, not just model quality, is becoming a key competitive KPI to track for AI startups versus incumbents.
insights.quote: you got a race like basically open anthropic or Gemini of like can you acrue so much proprietary data that you get the flywheel to escape velocity before the big boys do it

insights.claim: Woo says he has heard that the entire public internet is roughly 30 trillion tokens, while leading labs like OpenAI and Anthropic reportedly used on the order of 200 trillion tokens of data for pretraining, implying most of their training data now comes from non-public, synthetic, or human-generated sources rather than the open web.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: The scarcity of public web data relative to what labs are actually consuming supports a structural bull case for human-data and synthetic-data suppliers as a picks-and-shovels play.

insights.claim: Frontier AI labs are reportedly paying $2,000 to $10,000 per task for human-generated agent traces and labeled data, with individual contractors sometimes paid around $1,000 an hour, pulling professionals like investment bankers and financial advisors out of their jobs to generate training data at roughly double their prior salary.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Human-data labor markets tied to AI training are becoming a real wage-competitive labor pool, a leading indicator of lab spend intensity worth tracking.
insights.quote: people are they're just spending like $2,000 to $10,000 a task

insights.claim: Woo notes that companies helping other companies shut down (he cites a portfolio company called Sunset) have discovered an emerging market for selling the intangible operational data of businesses that are winding down, treating a defunct company's historical operating data as a sellable AI-training asset.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Company-shutdown and data-brokerage services are an overlooked adjacent beneficiary of the AI training-data boom.

insights.claim: Woo argues the next frontier of training-data value is 'intent data' — not just an artifact like a lawyer's redlined document, but the human's stated reasoning for the edit — because models need to be trained on the 'why,' which cannot be scraped and must be purpose-generated.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Businesses that can capture reasoning/intent alongside raw work-product have a data moat that generic document scraping cannot replicate.
insights.quote: that intent data almost has to be specifically created for model training

insights.claim: Because well-funded model labs can simply 'throw money' at data problems, resource-constrained AI startups are forced into synthetic-data innovation earlier out of necessity, turning a funding constraint into a competitive advantage.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Capital-constrained AI startups may develop more efficient synthetic-data pipelines than well-capitalized labs, a potential technical moat worth diligencing.
insights.quote: the constraint should breed innovation if you do it right

insights.claim: Woo says Sam Altman told him at a private dinner that OpenAI had bought roughly 40% of the world's supply of memory following a trip to Korea, a data point Woo received before it became public knowledge and which confirmed his early conviction in the 'memory trade.'
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Direct founder/executive access surfaced the memory-supply-crunch thesis roughly a year before it became consensus and priced into Micron/SK Hynix.
insights.quote: I had a dinner with Sam Alman at OpenAI and he just mentioned that like he came back from Korea. He like bought like 40% of the world's supply of memory.

insights.claim: Woo believes the memory trade (Micron, SK Hynix) is now fairly well understood and priced in the near term, but the open question is whether AI-driven memory demand is structural or cyclical; he leans toward Micron being undervalued since it trades cheaper on a multiple basis now than earlier in the year despite the stock's run.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: A cheaper forward multiple despite a large stock price run suggests the memory trade may not be as crowded as headline returns imply.
insights.quote: Micron is cheaper now than it was at the beginning of the year if I if I remember correctly.

insights.claim: Woo speculates that if humanoid robot deployment scales toward billions of units, memory demand could grow up to 100x current levels, potentially making Micron and SK Hynix into multi-trillion-dollar companies, though he is uncertain whether the market is early or late to this structural shift.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: A humanoid-driven memory demand supercycle is a multi-year thesis worth monitoring as robot production volumes scale.
insights.quote: if we really see like more humanoids than humans, I think you still have 100x more memory to go, right?

insights.claim: Woo observes a shift in AI inference infrastructure from single-agent, GPU-heavy workloads (roughly an 8:1 GPU-to-CPU ratio) toward multi-agent orchestration where each agent runs on its own container/cluster, requiring proportionally more CPUs — a dynamic he says is already showing up in Intel and AMD trading, with portfolio company Cognition's cloud coding agents as an example of the orchestration pattern.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: A shift toward multi-agent orchestration could rebalance AI infrastructure spend from pure GPU demand toward CPU-heavy compute, a potential tailwind for Intel/AMD relative to Nvidia.
insights.quote: maybe it's instead of like 8:1 GPU to CPU, maybe it gets one to one CPU to GPU.

insights.claim: Woo expects new entrants like Etched (founded by Gavin and Rob, targeting Nvidia inference dominance) to emerge and capture a share of the outsized memory/compute profit pool, predicting one to three credible new technical teams will challenge the choke point alongside opportunistic 'grift' attempts by others without real technology.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: The scale of profits sitting with Micron/SK Hynix/Nvidia is large enough to attract credible new competitors, a dynamic worth tracking for both upside challengers and public-market hype/grift plays.
insights.quote: we are credibly think we can go, you know, relieve this choke point. And if we do, we can make billions and billions of dollars.

insights.claim: Woo argues that as AI commoditizes software development (via coding agents), competitive advantage is shifting from Silicon-Valley-style capex-light software models toward capex-heavy 'atoms' businesses like data centers, chips, and power, reversing decades of preference for asset-light business models.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Investors historically biased toward asset-light software should reconsider capital-intensive infrastructure plays as a new source of durable moat in an AI-commoditized software world.
insights.quote: everyone's buying data centers, everyone's buying ships, everyone's buying power

insights.claim: Woo frames electricity availability and cost ('watts') as the ultimate AI bottleneck once chips, models, and applications are assumed to be good, which he says explains rising interest in unconventional power sources like space-based clusters, ocean-based clusters, and nuclear clusters to feed AI data centers.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Power generation and grid capacity are becoming the binding constraint on AI scaling, making energy infrastructure a higher-leverage investment than another layer of chips or models.
insights.quote: everything is just like the denomination of watts now, right?

insights.claim: Tungsten is an underappreciated choke-point commodity: it is used for thermal management in high-end semiconductor/GPU packaging (to prevent melting under high compute density) and in fusion reactor components, and China holds the highest-purity tungsten concentrate and has begun restricting exports.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: China's tungsten export restrictions are a supply-chain risk for both current AI chip production and future fusion energy buildout, a commodity worth watching for price spikes.
insights.quote: China has like the highest purity concentrate of tungsten. They're starting to like shut down exports of that because they know that's a choke point.

insights.claim: Woo is a direct investor in fusion startup Helion and, based on visiting its reactor prototype, believes the company is close to breakthroughs that could soon be published, reinforcing his view that fusion energy is now an investable near-term reality rather than a purely speculative long-horizon bet.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Direct diligence access to fusion prototypes suggests fusion timelines may be compressing faster than public consensus expects, relevant for long-duration energy and power-hungry AI infrastructure plays.
insights.quote: I think they're close to some breakthroughs and hopefully will be published and all of that stuff.

insights.claim: Woo highlights that top investors like Peter Thiel/Founders Fund succeed by holding multiple interlocking bets on the same underlying choke point (data center capacity spanning ocean-based compute and SpaceX's orbital data ambitions), capturing information asymmetry and first-look access across an entire theme rather than picking a single company.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Rather than picking a single winner in a sector, investors can replicate top-fund strategy by building a portfolio of complementary bets across one choke-point theme.
insights.quote: whoever solves the data center problem, Peter and founders fund is going to make a lot of money.

insights.claim: Woo cites portfolio company Efronence (purpose-built robot cameras) as a second-order choke point: current camera suppliers spun out of Intel's RealSense can only produce a few hundred units a month, forcing most robotics and data-collection teams to rely on repurposed consumer hardware like Meta glasses, GoPros, and iPhones.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Purpose-built sensor/camera supply for robotics data collection is a manufacturing bottleneck distinct from the chip/memory choke points, and a candidate picks-and-shovels investment in the humanoid robotics buildout.
insights.quote: the leading camera providers are like real sensors spun out of Intel they can only make like a few hundred cameras a month. So there's like a huge choke point there.

insights.claim: A robotics founder in Woo's network was, roughly 6-12 months before this conversation, personally sourcing recycled DRAM chips scavenged from old computer boards from Chinese suppliers due to memory scarcity — an early, ground-level signal of the memory shortage that later became a consensus 'memory trade.'
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Founder-level operational pain points (needing to buy scavenged components) can precede consensus macro trades by 6-12 months, making founder conversations a leading indicator.
insights.quote: there's like Chinese suppliers who like rip old DRAM chips off of like old computer boards and like resell them.

insights.claim: Woo's defense-investing thesis (Antifund holds stakes in Anduril and other defense startups) rests on the view that legacy defense primes are culturally slow — citing multi-month planning cycles and quarterly board-meeting cadences at legacy contractors — causing top engineering talent to migrate to venture-backed 'neo-primes,' similar to how SpaceX pulled the best talent out of legacy aerospace.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Talent migration from legacy defense primes to newer venture-backed contractors is a leading indicator of where defense-tech market share will shift over the next decade.
insights.quote: they basically just become these magnets of anyone who's worth anything in the industry and wants to go work for them anyways.

insights.claim: Woo predicts a market will emerge for weaponized humanoid robots for military use, even though major public humanoid makers (Tesla, Figure, 1X, Agility Robotics, and Chinese makers like Unitree) have publicly committed not to arm their robots, because he says at least one unnamed humanoid company is deliberately pursuing weaponization as a core use case.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: A humanoid company willing to build weaponized robots for the military could capture an entire underserved defense-tech niche that publicly-committed competitors are avoiding.
insights.quote: I know of one humanoid company I'm not going to say their name who is taking the exact opposite approach

insights.claim: Woo argues autonomous targeting of human targets by drones is likely already occurring in active conflicts (referencing Ukraine/Russia), framing drone-on-drone and drone-on-human autonomous warfare as an inevitable near-term reality rather than a speculative future risk.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Autonomous-weapons capability is moving faster than public policy debate suggests, relevant to defense-tech investment timing and regulatory risk.
insights.quote: there's going to be autonomous targeting of human targets by robots.

insights.claim: Despite holding Bitcoin since 2013, Woo says he is now considering trimming his position because he cannot clearly articulate what tail-risk scenario Bitcoin actually hedges — arguing that if the US dollar or government were to fail, he would rather hold bullets, water, and land than Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: A long-time OG Bitcoin holder publicly questioning Bitcoin's hedging logic and trimming exposure is a notable sentiment data point among crypto-native investors.
insights.quote: if all the US stuff is bad then I probably want bullets, water, land, like just actual like survivable stuff.

insights.claim: Woo has rotated a portion of his crypto allocation from Bitcoin toward Hyperliquid, citing its clearer cash-flow-generating business model (fee capture from trading/leverage activity plus token buybacks) versus Bitcoin's harder-to-underwrite 'digital gold' store-of-value narrative.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Investors comparing crypto assets on cash-flow fundamentals rather than narrative may find protocols like Hyperliquid more analytically tractable than Bitcoin.
insights.quote: I've rotated a lot towards hyperlquid cuz I understand the use case.

insights.claim: Woo states that market-wide risk capital has rotated out of Bitcoin into AI over the past year because AI assets currently offer more yield, productivity narrative, and better realized returns, explicitly noting that buying Bitcoin a year ago produced very different (worse) outcomes than buying AI exposure.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Capital rotation from Bitcoin to AI narratives has been a measurable performance driver over the trailing year and may continue until AI returns cool off.
insights.quote: if you bought Bitcoin a year ago versus bought AI, like there are very different outcomes.

insights.claim: Woo argues GLP-1 drugs are becoming a significant, underappreciated force reducing gambling, drinking, and partying behavior at a societal level, citing that multiple hedge funds have banned traders from taking them because the drugs reduce risk-taking and aggression, though he doesn't yet see the effect as large enough to be a standalone tradeable thesis.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Widespread GLP-1 adoption could be a slow-moving macro tailwind against consumer discretionary categories tied to vice spending (alcohol, gambling, fast food) worth monitoring as a long-term thesis.
insights.quote: I think everyone will be micro doing GLP-1s in the future.

insights.claim: Woo predicts compulsive gambling driven by betting and prediction-market apps will become a 'huge national epidemic,' framing it as a symptom of broader economic hopelessness among people who feel they cannot escape low-wage work, channeling that desperation into meme-stock and prediction-market speculation.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Rising gambling-app and prediction-market penetration is a social/regulatory risk that could eventually draw policy intervention, similar to past crackdowns on other addictive consumer products.
insights.quote: I am very convinced that the gambling in particular is going to be this like huge national epidemic. It may already be there, but like it it's going to get worse.

insights.claim: Woo argues respect, not money, is the ultimate currency of power — observing that wealthy people without social standing or respect get little deference in rooms that matter, and that the ideal position is being highly respected within an influential inner circle while remaining relatively unknown to the broader public.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: A useful heuristic for evaluating founders and operators: track who commands respect among insiders, which is often a better signal of durable influence than public fame or net worth alone.
insights.quote: if you don't have respect but you have money, the money is no good in the rooms that you want to be in.

insights.claim: Woo describes observing Donald Trump at the White House deliberately pause and stand still upon entering a room to visibly command attention before proceeding, framing it as a masterclass in interpersonal power dynamics independent of his politics.
insights.speaker: Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifund
insights.so_what: Illustrates how Woo evaluates high-status individuals on tactical mastery of influence and attention, a transferable skill he suggests founders and operators can study regardless of political alignment.
insights.quote: He walks in a room and he stops. He just stands in the door and he just looks and everyone looks at him, right?
